text: The surgical team prepares for a laparoscopic right colectomy on a patient with a tattoo marking the location of a recently diagnosed cecal tumor. Three ports are set up: a 5mm port in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm umbilical port for the camera, and a 12mm suprapubic port.

The video shows a laparoscopic setup for a right colectomy on a patient with a tattoo marking the location of a recently diagnosed cecal tumor. Three ports are used: a 5mm port in the left upper quadrant, a 10mm camera port through the umbilical region due to an umbilical hernia, and a 12mm port in the suprapubic area.

The surgeon tents the ileocolic pedicle, undoing folds of the mesentery to expose the base of the pedicle. A medial to lateral dissection is initiated beneath the ileocolic pedicle, with the duodenum becoming visible and swept downward. The peritoneum around the ileocolic pedicle is opened.
The surgeon tents the ileocolic pedicle, carefully undoing folds of the mesentery to expose the true base of the pedicle for further dissection.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection beneath the ileocolic pedicle, with the duodenum becoming visible and being swept down. The peritoneum around the ileocolic pedicle is opened.

The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and vein, sweeping the duodenum down to prevent injury. The ileocolic vein is clipped before division, and the artery is prepared for isolation, with visible clearance of fatty tissue around the vascular pedicle.

I think whether or not you do a CME dissection or just a standard high ligation of your vascular pedicles, it's really important to really sweep the duodenum down and to completely isolate your pedicle and clear it of all of its fatty tissue to ensure there's no inadvertent injury of the duodenum or inadvertently not doing a high ligation.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic artery and vein, with the vein visible first. Clips are applied to the vein before division for added security, followed by the isolation of the artery.

The surgeon identifies bulky lymph nodes within the transected mesentery and continues medial dissection along the expected path of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The dissection proceeds underneath the mesentery along the visible white line, utilizing a bloodless plane that separates easily without sharp dissection.

The surgeon observes residual lymph tissue and bulky lymph nodes in the transected mesentery, emphasizing the importance of cleaning the area thoroughly. The medial dissection continues, progressing upwards.

The surgeon performs medial dissection underneath the mesentery along the white line, revealing a bloodless plane that separates easily without sharp dissection. The expected location of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is indicated.
Using three laparoscopic ports, the surgeon provides adequate exposure to the surgical field. The head of the pancreas is visible as the surgeon thins out the mesentery to isolate the blood vessel.

Using three ports, the surgeon provides adequate exposure and proceeds to dissect upwards towards the right colic vessels. The head of the pancreas is visible, and the surgeon begins thinning the mesentery to isolate the blood vessel.
The surgeon isolates the artery and vein, clipping and dividing each vessel. The area is cleared of surrounding fat to minimize residual bleeding and ensure controlled vessel division.

The surgeon isolates the artery and vein, clipping and dividing each vessel. The artery is visible and clipped before division, followed by the vein, ensuring minimal residual bleeding by removing surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon continues medial dissection over the duodenum towards the hepatic flexure, aiming to bring the right branch of the middle colic artery into view. The dissection progresses through a wad of tissue, identifying the surgical margin without completely isolating the artery.

The surgeon continues the medial dissection over the duodenum and up to the hepatic flexure, aiming to bring the right branch of the middle colic artery into view. The surgical field shows the identification of the margin for the resection.

The surgeon navigates through a wad of tissue containing the right branch of the middle colic artery, without fully isolating it. The planned colon margin is identified, and a clip is placed for future reference.

The surgeon identifies the planned colon margin and places a clip for later identification. The omentum is being split at the level of the planned colon margin, preparing it to be taken with the specimen.
The surgeon removes the omentum along with the specimen during the right colectomy procedure, following the technique they were taught.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned colon margin and proceeds with the lateral dissection. The previous medial dissection extends over the pancreas and duodenum, simplifying the current lateral dissection by reducing it to a few cell layers of tissue.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection after completing a full medial dissection over the pancreas and duodenum, using a ligature device. The dissection proceeds around the cecal attachments, with the final lateral attachments being taken from an inferior angle.

The surgeon uses a ligature to perform a full medial and lateral dissection, navigating around the cecal attachments. The final lateral attachments are taken from an inferior angle for better access and ease.

The surgeon completes the lateral attachments and proceeds to dissect the small bowel mesentery. The mesentery is folded on itself, allowing simultaneous dissection of both leaflets, though the tissue appears thick.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the cecum and terminal ileum, unfolding the mesentery. The small bowel mesentery is then transected, with both leaflets being taken simultaneously due to the unfolded position.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization, unfolding the mesentery, and prepares for bowel transection. A perfusion assessment is conducted using ICG mode to ensure adequate blood flow at the planned transection sites. The stapler is aligned with the bowel and used to transect the colon, followed by the small bowel, through the suprapubic port.

The surgeon completes the medial and lateral attachments and prepares to transect the bowel. A perfusion assessment is conducted using ICG mode to ensure adequate blood flow at the planned transection sites. The stapler is aligned with the bowel and the colon is transected, followed by the small bowel. All stapling is performed through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen extraction.

The surgeon places the divided specimen into the pelvis and attaches a stay suture on the stapled end of the colon to the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal. A suture passer is used to stabilize the isoparastaltic anastomosis. The surgeon checks the mesentery for twisting and creates a colotomy using a Maryland instrument, followed by an enterotomy.

The surgeon places the divided specimen into the pelvis and attaches a stay suture on the stapled end of the colon to the small bowel, approximately seven centimeters proximal. A suture passer is used to stabilize the isoparastaltic anastomosis. The surgeon checks the mesentery for twisting and performs a colotomy using a Maryland instrument, followed by an enterotomy.
The surgeon carefully staples through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler to secure the common channel, avoiding excessive pressure to prevent penetrating the back wall of the bowel.
The surgeon carefully staples through the suprapubic port using a vascular load stapler to secure the common channel, ensuring not to penetrate the back wall of the bowel during the process.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy and enterotomy. Using a barbed suture, the surgeon begins closure at the near corner, employing a transition stitch and a baseball suture technique to ensure secure closure. The barbed suture maintains tension with each cinch, facilitating a consistent closure.

The surgeon aligns the colon and small bowel to minimize the size of the colotomy or common channel enterotomy. A barbed suture is used, starting at the near corner, to ensure a secure closure of the anastomosis.

The surgeon cinches the suture after each bite, performing a transition stitch around the near corner. The suturing technique resembles a baseball stitch as the surgeon sews away from themselves, ensuring the lumen is not narrowed in this location.

The surgeon applies a second layer of sutures using a barbed suture, ensuring consistent tension with each cinch. The choice of suture material, whether Vicryl, PDS, or barbed, is less critical than maintaining proper spacing and tension of the suture bites.

The surgeon uses a nine-inch V-lock suture to perform a Lembert stitch, ensuring proper spacing and tension. The suture technique effectively buries the first layer, with the second layer providing additional security.
The surgeon takes a bite out past the corner of the anastomosis before coming back down, preparing to apply a Lembert stitch for the next layer using a nine-inch V-lock suture.

The surgeon sutures the second layer of the anastomosis, ensuring the first layer is buried. The near corner is carefully checked by opening the tissue to confirm complete closure, sometimes using a silk suture as a target.

The surgeon carefully sutures down to the near corner, ensuring visibility by opening the area to check the suture placement. A silk suture is occasionally used as a target for closure. The V-lock suture is secured with clips using a clip applier, and the suture tethering is cut, concluding the operation.
The surgeon ends the V-lock suture by running it back a couple of throws and applying clips with a clip applier to secure the closure. The suture tethering is then cut, concluding the operation.
